scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE CITY CIVIL COURT
AT BENGALURU

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

Anita Sharma,
Aged about 52 years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Indiranagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

Rajesh Reddy,
Aged about 60 years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: HSR Layout, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF CONTRACT RELATING TO IMMOVABLE PROPERTY WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant resides within such territorial limits.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. 85,00,000/- (Rupees Eighty Five Lakhs Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Court.

3A. The Plaintiff states that the mandatory pre-institution mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 has been complied with, as the suit value exceeds the specified threshold for commercial disputes.

LIMITATION

4. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from the date fixed for performance, or if no date is fixed, when the Plaintiff has notice that performance is refused. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

FACTS OF THE CASE

5. On 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into an Agreement to Sell regarding the residential property bearing Flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft. The total sale consideration was fixed at Rs. 85,00,000/-. The said Agreement to Sell is marked as Annexure A.

6. Pursuant to the terms of the Agreement to Sell, the Plaintiff paid an advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- to the Defendant on 10.01.2024 itself. The payment was made via RTGS bearing UTR No. HDFC20240110987654. The proof of payment is marked as Annexure B.

7. As per the agreed terms, the balance sale consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- was to be paid by the Plaintiff on or before 10.07.2024 against the execution and registration of the Sale Deed. The Plaintiff has always been ready and willing to perform her part of the contractual obligations.

8. The Plaintiff maintained the necessary funds to pay the balance consideration and repeatedly requested the Defendant to schedule the execution of the Sale Deed. The Plaintiff remains ready and willing to pay the balance consideration and complete the transaction as per the Agreement to Sell.

BREACH OF CONTRACT

9. On 05.06.2024, prior to the deadline for balance payment, the Defendant issued a letter to the Plaintiff expressly refusing to execute the Sale Deed. The Defendant stated in the said letter that he intended to sell the property to another party who had made a higher offer. The said refusal letter is marked as Annexure C.

10. Upon receipt of the Defendant's refusal, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to honour the Agreement to Sell and execute the Sale Deed. The legal notice along with the postal receipt is marked as Annexure D.

11. Despite the service of the legal notice and the Plaintiff's continued readiness to perform, the Defendant has failed and refused to execute the Sale Deed. The Defendant has wrongfully withheld the property from the Plaintiff in breach of the contractual terms.

DAMAGES

12. Due to the Defendant's wrongful refusal and breach of contract, the Plaintiff has been deprived of the possession and ownership of the suit property. The Plaintiff has suffered prejudice as the property is a specific residential flat suited to her requirements.

13. The Defendant's conduct has necessitated the present litigation, causing the Plaintiff to incur legal expenses and costs. The Plaintiff is entitled to recover the costs of the suit and interest on the advance amount paid due to the wrongful detention of funds by the Defendant.

LEGAL BASIS

14. The present cause is governed by the Specific Relief Act, 1963, including Section 10 (mandatory after 2018 Amendment), Section 16, Section 21, and Section 22.

15. The present cause is governed by the Indian Contract Act, 1872, including Section 10.

16. The Plaintiff also relies on the Transfer of Property Act, 1882, including Section 53A, where the pleaded facts attract those provisions.

CAUSE OF ACTION

17. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant committed the breach giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to institute the present suit.

18. The cause of action further arose when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice dated 20.06.2024, failed and refused to comply with the Plaintiff's demand.

19. The cause of action is not a continuing cause of action. The breach consisted of a single event. However, the Plaintiff's right to damages subsists.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

20. The suit is valued at Rs. 85,00,000/- (Rupees Eighty Five Lakhs Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Appropriate court fee has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Court Fees Act, 1870 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

21. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment / decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (b) Future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment / decree till the date of realisation of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree for specific performance of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024, directing the Defendant to execute and register the Sale Deed in favour of the Plaintiff upon payment of the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/-;
    (b) In the alternative, award compensation/damages for breach of contract if specific performance is not granted;
    (c) Award pendente lite and future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems just under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (d) Award costs of the suit including costs of the legal notice;
    (e) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Original agreement dated 10.01.2024

Annexure B — RTGS receipt (UTR: HDFC20240110987654)

Annexure C — Defendant's refusal letter dated 05.06.2024

Annexure D — Legal notice dated 20.06.2024 with postal receipt

VERIFICATION

Verified at Bengaluru on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, Anita Sharma, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 12 of this plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and no material facts have been concealed therefrom, and that I have not filed any other suit, petition, or proceeding in any court or before any authority in respect of the same subject matter and cause of action.

DEPONENT

Anita Sharma

PLAINTIFF

                                    THROUGH:
                                    {{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
                                    Advocate for the Plaintiff
                                    Enrollment No. {{ENROLLMENT_NO}}
                                    {{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE COMMERCIAL COURT (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT BENGALURU

Commercial Suit No. __________ / 20____

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A company registered under the Companies Act,
Having its registered office at MG Road, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Director Priya Menon, aged 40 years.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A limited liability partnership having its office at Whitefield, Bengaluru,
Represented by its Designated Partner Vikram Singh, aged 48 years.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

COMMERCIAL COURT MAINTAINABILITY

1. The subject matter of the present suit arises out of a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

2. The suit is valued above the specified value as prescribed under Section 12 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

JURISDICTION

3. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

4. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant carries on business within such territorial limits.

5. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court. This Hon'ble Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain commercial disputes under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

LIMITATION

6. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from when the contract is broken. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

7. The Plaintiff states that pre-institution mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 has been completed. The mediation was conducted before the Karnataka State Legal Services Authority on {{MEDIATION_DATE}} and the outcome certificate is annexed hereto. Alternatively, if mediation was not conducted, the Plaintiff is seeking urgent interim relief which exempts compliance under Section 12A(1).

FACTS OF THE CASE

8. The Plaintiff states that on 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into a Software Development Agreement for the development of an ERP system. The total contract value was fixed at Rs. 45,00,000/-. A copy of the said Agreement is annexed hereto as Annexure A.

9. Pursuant to the terms of the Agreement, the Plaintiff paid a sum of Rs. 20,00,000/- to the Defendant as the first milestone payment on 01.02.2024. The payment was made via NEFT bearing UTR Number ICIC20240201456789. A copy of the NEFT receipt is annexed hereto as Annexure B.

10. As per the agreed timeline under the Agreement, the Defendant was obligated to deliver the first module of the ERP system on or before 01.05.2024.

11. The Defendant failed to deliver the first module by the agreed deadline of 01.05.2024. Despite reminders, no deliverables were provided by the Defendant.

12. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant communicated its decision to abandon the project entirely. The Defendant has failed to return the advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- despite the abandonment of the contract. Copies of the email correspondence evidencing the abandonment are annexed hereto as Annexure C.

13. The Plaintiff issued a legal notice to the Defendant dated 01.07.2024 calling upon the Defendant to refund the advance and compensate for the losses suffered. The Defendant has failed to comply with the said notice. A copy of the legal notice is annexed hereto as Annexure E.

DAMAGES

14. Due to the breach of contract and abandonment of the project, the Plaintiff has suffered actual loss comprising the refund of the advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- and consequential loss of Rs. 12,00,000/- due to delay in operations. The consequential loss has been assessed by an independent auditor. A copy of the loss assessment report is annexed hereto as Annexure D. The total claim for damages amounts to Rs. 32,00,000/-.

15. The Plaintiff is entitled to interest on the withheld amount and damages at the rate of 18% per annum from the date of breach until realisation.

LEGAL BASIS

16. The Defendant has committed a repudiatory breach by refusing to perform its obligations, entitling the Plaintiff to treat the contract as at an end and seek appropriate relief.

17. The Plaintiff is entitled to compensation under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which provides that when a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive compensation for any loss or damage caused thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach, or which the parties knew, when they made the contract, to be likely to result from the breach of it.

CAUSE OF ACTION

18. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant abandoned the project and committed breach of the Software Development Agreement, giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to institute the present suit.

19. The cause of action is continuing in nature as the Defendant continues to withhold the advance payment and has failed to compensate for the losses suffered.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

20. The suit is valued at Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Appropriate court fee has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Court Fees Act, 1870 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

21. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Interest at 18% per annum from 15.06.2024 until the date of filing of this suit;
    (b) Pendente lite interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment/decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (c) Future interest at such rate as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment/decree till the date of realisation of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Hon'ble Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for a sum of Rs. 32,00,000/- (Rupees Thirty-Two Lakhs Only) towards damages;
    (b) Award Rs. 20,00,000/- towards refund of advance payment;
    (c) Award Rs. 12,00,000/- towards consequential loss/damages;
    (d) Award interest at 18% per annum from 15.06.2024 until realisation;
    (e) Award costs of the suit including costs of the legal notice;
    (f) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Hon'ble Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

SCHEDULE OF DAMAGES

The Plaintiff claims the following heads of damages:

22. Refund of Advance Payment
    Amount claimed: Rs. 20,00,000/-
    Basis: First milestone payment made on 01.02.2024, not returned despite contract abandonment
    Documentary proof: Annexure B (NEFT receipt)

23. Consequential Loss/Damages
    Amount claimed: Rs. 12,00,000/-
    Basis: Loss due to delay in operations caused by non-delivery of ERP system
    Documentary proof: Annexure D (Loss assessment report by auditor)

TOTAL DAMAGES CLAIMED: Rs. 32,00,000/-

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Software Development Agreement dated 01.02.2024

Annexure B — NEFT receipt (UTR: ICIC20240201456789)

Annexure C — Email correspondence showing abandonment

Annex
